attributes.stem: A 58-year-old woman is admitted to hospital for a wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy following a diagnosis of breast carcinoma one week ago. On history, it is found that she is on dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel), which she has been taking since the insertion of two coronary artery stents two years ago following a myocardial infarction. Other regular medications include atorvastatin, perindopril, daily calcium and vitamin-D supplements.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of action?
options: Cancel surgery and complete treatment with chemotherapy
Stop clopidogrel and operate in one week
Stop aspirin and operate in one week
Stop aspirin and clopidogrel and operate in one week
attributes.explanation:
It is important to balance the risks and benefits of dual anti-platelet therapy in peri-operative setting to ensure good patient outcome. There are several things to consider when deciding whether a patient should continue their dual anti-platelet therapy before a surgical procedure or not. This includes the type of surgery, past history of major cerebrovascular incident (including myocardial infarction and stroke), as well as the urgency of the surgery.

Australian guidelines currently suggest that following acute coronary syndrome treated with stenting, dual antiplatelet therapy should be continued for 12 months. Following 12 months, a risk assessment needs to be performed to establish the risk of haemorrhage and balance this with the cardiovascular benefit. Generally, aspirin should be continued indefinitely.

In this case, due to the patient's history of myocardial infarction requiring insertion of coronary artery stents, as well as the lower risk of major bleeding during a wide-local excision, it is reasonable to continue her on aspirin pre-operatively to reduce risks of cardiovascular events. However, clopidogrel should be stopped to reduce risks of bleeding.

Clopidogrel is recommended to be discontinued one week prior to surgery. Surgery is not an urgent matter in this case and can easily be delayed for a week without compromising good management of the breast cancer. It is inappropriate to proceed to surgery as planned as the dual antiplatelet increases the bleeding risk of the surgery.

Cancel surgery and complete treatment with chemotherapy is not an appropriate answer as it compromises the patient's safety in regards to her breast cancer management.
learningPoints: Aspirin can be continued in high-risk patients (e.g., history of MI/stroke) undergoing non-cardiac surgery as it reduces the risk of adverse cardiac events without increasing bleeding complications.
It is vital to balance the risk of thromboembolism against the risk of bleeding in peri-operative period.
references: O’Riordan JM, Margey RJ, Blake G, O’Connell PR. Antiplatelet Agents in the Perioperative Period. Arch Surg. 2009;144(1):69–76. doi:10.1001/archsurg.144.1.69

Oscarsson, A. Gupta, M. Fredrikson, J. Järhult, M. Nyström, E. Pettersson, B. Darvish, H. Krook, E. Swahn, C. Eintrei, To continue or discontinue aspirin in the perioperative period: a randomized, controlled clinical trial, BJA: British Journal of Anaesthesia, Volume 104, Issue 3, March 2010, Pages 305–312, https://doi.org/10.1093/bja/aeq003
Gerstein NS, Albrechtsen CL, Mercado N, Cigarroa JE, Schulman PM. A comprehensive update on aspirin management during noncardiac surgery. Anesthesia & Analgesia. 2020 Oct 1;131(4):1111-23
